turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg has our opinion this morning in case 15-457, Microsoft Corporation versus Baker.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: The question presented in this case, when a district judge denies class action certification, what immediate recourse to an appeal may that would be class representative obtained? Orders granting or denying class certification can be altered any time up to the entry of final judgment. They are, therefore, inherently interlocutory. That means that, under the Final Judgment Rule, codified in §1291 of the Judicial Code, class action status rulings are not immediately appealable. Denial of class certification, however, sometimes leaves plaintiffs with little incentive to pursue their individual claims, thereby effectively ending the lawsuit. Imagine a $100.00 individual claim not worth pursuing alone but worthwhile if it can be pursued on behalf of a class of thousands. Various approaches to this dilemma have been tried. In the Interlocutory Appeals Act of 1958, 28 U.S.C. §1292(b), Congress allowed immediate appeal of an adverse class certification ruling if certain requirements are met. The ruling must involve a question on which there is substantial ground for disagreement, and the appealing party must obtain permission for the appeal twice, first from the district court, and next from the court of appeals. Concerned, perhaps, that §1292(b) is too restrictive courts of appeals developed the so-called Death-Knell Doctrine. That doctrine made an order denying class certification instantly appealable if, in the court of appeals assessment, the order sounded the litigation's death-knell. In 1978, in Coopers & Lybrand against Livesay, this Court unanimously rejected the Death-Knell Doctrine as inconsistent with §1291's firm final-judgment rule. The potential for multiple interlocutory appeals and attendant delay and disruption, we explained inhered in the doctrine. The Death-Knell theory, moreover, thwarted the two-level permission requirement Congress had established for interlocutory appeals in §1292(b). The doctrine was also one-sided. It enabled only plaintiffs to secure immediate review of an unfavorable certification order. Yet class certification can be a vitally important issue for the defendant as well. Two decades after Coopers & Lybrand, in 1998, a new avenue opened for immediate appeal of certification orders. This Court approved Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 23(f) which authorizes interlocutory appeals from adverse class certification orders in the sole discretion of the court of appeals. In contrast to §1292(b), Rule 23(f) requires only one level of permission to appeal a class action ruling. Yet another approach was essayed in this case, an attempt to secure as of right an appeal from an order denying class certification. Plaintiffs below, respondents here, filed a punitive class action against Microsoft alleging a design defect in its popular videogame console, the Xbox 360. The district court struck the class allegations from the complaint, and the Court of Appeals denied permission to appeal that order at once. The class action seekers then voluntarily dismissed their claims with prejudice and appealed, challenging only the interlocutory order striking their class allegations. If they prevailed on appeal, they urged, their claims would be revived. The Ninth Circuit held it had jurisdiction to entertain the appeal as one from a judgment qualifying as final under §1291. We now reverse the Ninth Circuit's disposition. §1291's final-judgment rule serves important interests. It minimizes harassment and delay, and promotes the efficient administration of justice. We have repeatedly resisted efforts to stretch §1291 to permit appeals of right that would erode the Finality Principle. We do so again today. Federal courts of appeals, we hold, lack authority under §1291 to review an order denying class certification. And that absence of authority persists when the named plaintiffs have voluntarily dismissed their claims with prejudice. The Voluntary Dismissal Device, even more than the Death-Knell Theory, invites protracted litigation and piecemeal appeals. Under that device, the decision whether an immediate appeal may be taken resides exclusively with the plaintiff, she need only dismiss her claims with prejudice in order to appeal the District Court's refusal to certify class. And she may do so again until the district court on remand denies certification on a different ground. Defendants objecting to class certification would have no access to this route. The device, moreover, would end-run Rule 23(f)'s carefully crafted interlocutory appeal avenue which gives courts of appeals discretion whether to allow instant appeal. In short, putative class action plaintiffs cannot transform a tentative interlocutory order into a final judgment within the meaning of §1291 simply by conditionally dismissing their claims with prejudice. Justice Thomas has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment in which the Chief Justice and Justice Alito have joined. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
